text: I have to admit that in my Ruby days, this was the only language where it happened to me surprisingly often that I would just write a piece of code without looking at the documentation thinking “I have no idea but I feel like this is how that should work” — and it did.

text: Ruby is so delightfully unsound: perpetually punting on precision and completeness, favoring a sort of joy and ease.

elements.text: Ruby is so delightfully unsound: perpetually punting on precision and completeness, favoring a sort of joy and ease.

text:
For example, here's one that broke me — just now.  After writing it thousands of times, I cannot type this idiom anymore:
```hash_mapping_x_to_fx = Hash[xs.map{|x| [x, f(x)]] ```
From now on, I'm going with:
```hash_mapping_x_to_fx = xs.hap{|x| f(x)}```
Remember "Ruby was a Lisp originally, in theory."  So the definition, for those unfamiliar, can be simple as:
```module Enumerable
  def hap
    Hash[map{|x| [x, yield(x)]}]
  end
end```

text: Interesting, thanks for sharing! I used to use Leo ages ago, but gave up on it at some point because OrgMode in Emacs was more convenient (mainly because I needed Emacs anyway). I was surprised to see that Leo is still alive (it was and still is an essentially one-person project), and even more to see that it is developing a tool ecosystem around it!

text: To tie this up, I ended up dropping the heatmap vis that was there and just went with a "disappearing key" effects for visualizing the history of the symbols. It was actually sort of interesting how the keyboard is sort of organized in rings with the innermost keys being the oldest and the newest keys being on the periphery. If only there was more time in the day :slightly_smiling_face:

text: It's a cool story and you've presented it well. I think it's a good name for a language! Building web apps still can be a hassle. What do you see the long term advantages might be? Do you think for web apps you really need a number of DSLS (frontend, routing, persistence, testing, deploying, etc)? What do you think the costs might be to develop the language tooling that would make this a net-win for users?

elements.text: It's a cool story and you've presented it well. I think it's a good name for a language! Building web apps still can be a hassle. What do you see the long term advantages might be? What do you think the costs might be to develop the language tooling that would make this a net-win for users?

elements.text: It's a cool story and you've presented it well. I think it's a good name for a language! Building web apps still can be a hassle. What do you see the long term advantages might be? Do you think for web apps you really need a number of DSLS (frontend, routing, persistence, testing, deploying, etc)? What do you think the costs might be to develop the language tooling that would make this a net-win for users?

text:
Thanks <@UMVFWPZ36>!

> long term advantages
Long term advantage would be code that is closer to specification, shorter and more maintainable. Also, easier to write, lowering the knowledge barier needed to build a web app and allowing faster prototyping.

> you really need a number of DSLS
I am not sure what exactly you mean by this, so pls correct me if I did not answer the question.
Idea of Wasp is that you get a lot of stuff done in a single DSL, that spans the concept of the whole web app. However, if there is something you need to customize in more details, and there is a good way out there to do it that people love, it probably makes more sense to do it that way then to reinvent the wheel in Wasp. Example of that is describing styling -> css is made for that and although it has its issues, people use it, and it would be too big task for us to reinvent it, so Wasp will not try to replace css, but instead allow using it. Same goes for html/jsx for layouting, React/Vue for reusable components, Javascript/TS for general / business logic.
Wasp as DSL serves the purpose of gluing this all together, removing unneeded complexity and hiding ugly/boring parts.

> language tooling
Good question! Since we are imagining it as a pretty horizontal DSL, I believe it will be very important to have a good way to discover options, so some kind of auto completion could help a lot. With LSP I am imagining this should not be so hard to develop for a couple of main editors, but I have never been developing language tooling so it is all a guess :).

I hope these answers are addressing the questions well - I would love to hear your opinions on these topics!

text:
>  Explicit is better than implicit.
Agreed yet many of the most popular languages are implicit including C++, Java, C#, Swift. In particular accessing the properties of an instance in a class method. This is then often required to be solved on many teams by a naming convention since the language itself didn't force the issue.

text:
<@UU261JPCH> I was unaware of GDevelop. Thanks for the link.

Blueprints are a fairly full features programming system. Full AAA games can be largely (but not completely) made in blueprints without needing to program in C++. Generally, I see teams use blueprints for rapid prototyping and for creating higher level abstractions to be used by non-programmers, such as technical artists or technical game designers.

<@UMUJX89KL> let's definitely chat more.
